meta.title: The Rules Under the Game
meta.subtitle: Choices, incentives, money, and Bitcoin
meta.house_style: No em dashes. No hype. No badges or scores. Student acts before any principle is named.

modules.title: You Can't Have Everything
modules.core_question: When you cannot do everything, what does choosing actually cost you?
modules.hook: It is Friday evening. You have twenty dollars, three hours, and one phone battery at forty percent. You have something due Monday, and you know that if you do not make progress tonight, the whole weekend turns into catch-up. Several things you want to do are all competing for the same evening. You cannot do all of them.
modules.instruction: Add things to your evening. Watch your money, time, and battery as you go. When you are ready, see how the night went.
modules.concept_text: Scarcity does not mean there is nothing. It means you cannot use the same money, time, or energy twice. The thing you give up to make a choice has a name: opportunity cost. It is the next best thing you could have done instead.
modules.money_connection: Money is one of these limited budgets, but not the only one. Time and energy are scarce too. Every budget you spend is a budget you cannot spend on something else.
modules.exit_takeaway: Scarcity does not mean there is nothing. It means every choice spends something you cannot spend twice.

reflection.predict: Before you start: which one thing do you think you will protect tonight, no matter what?
reflection.explain: After your night: what did your choice protect, and what did it quietly cost you?
reflection.compare: Someone with the same twenty dollars but a different life might choose differently. Name one person you know who would, and why.

modules.pattern: You spent real budgets, and spending one closed off others. The cost was never visible at the moment you chose. It only showed up afterward.

modules.title: Why the Same Thing Is Worth More to One Person Than Another
modules.core_question: Does value live in the object, or in the person and the moment?
modules.hook: A phone charger is worth almost nothing when your battery is at one hundred percent. It is worth a lot at two percent. Same charger. What changed?
modules.instruction: Pick an object and a situation. Make a quick prediction about how much it is worth. Then reveal the reading and see why the situation moved the value.
modules.concept_text: Things have properties. A charger charges. Water hydrates. But the value of a thing is not stored inside it. People assign value based on who they are and what their situation is right now. Economists call this subjective value.
modules.money_connection: This is why a small amount of saved money can feel almost pointless on a normal day and suddenly matter a lot the day a plan breaks. The money did not change. The situation did.
modules.bitcoin_connection: Hold this thought. It comes back later when we ask why people in some places guard a unit of money far more carefully than people in others.
modules.exit_takeaway: Objects have properties. People assign value based on their situation.

reflection.predict: Pick one object below and guess: in which situation is it worth the most?
reflection.explain: After exploring a few: in your own words, where does value actually come from?
reflection.compare: Name one thing that is worth a lot to you and little to a friend. What is different, the thing or the moment?

modules.pattern: The object never changed. Only the situation around it did, and the value moved with the situation, not with the object.

modules.title: Invisible Rules Make People Move
modules.core_question: When behavior changes, is it the people who changed, or the rule they were under?
modules.hook: Why do people suddenly care about grades the week before report cards? The people did not become different people. Something about the rule changed what was worth doing.
modules.instruction: Switch on a classroom rule. First predict who will change their behavior. Then reveal who benefits, who pays, and what happens over time.
modules.concept_text: An incentive is a reward or a punishment that changes what people do. People mostly respond to the incentive in front of them, not to what someone hoped they would do. Change the rule and you change the behavior, even with the exact same people.
modules.money_connection: Money systems are full of incentives too. Later we will see how a rule about money can quietly reward spending fast, or reward saving, without anyone announcing it.
modules.exit_takeaway: When enough people face the same incentive for long enough, a pattern appears. The pattern is the rule's doing, not proof that the people are good or bad.

reflection.predict: Pick a rule. Before revealing: who do you think changes their behavior first?
reflection.explain: After a few rules: finish this sentence. Bad outcomes often come from ___, not from ___.
reflection.compare: Name a real rule at your school that produces a result nobody actually wants. What is it quietly rewarding?

modules.pattern: The same people behaved differently under a different rule. The behavior followed the incentive, not the character of the people.

modules.title: When the Scoreboard Lies
modules.core_question: What happens to people's decisions when the thing they measure with can be quietly changed?
modules.hook: Imagine a game where the referee can add points to one team while the game is still being played. Did that team get better, or did the scoreboard stop telling the truth?
modules.instruction: Pick a scenario. Adjust the scoreboard. Watch the gap open between what the scoreboard says and what is actually happening, and how people respond to the number instead of the truth.
modules.concept_text: Money is not only something to spend. It is a measuring stick for time, work, savings, and plans. People read that stick to decide what to do. If the stick quietly changes length, people are not being foolish when they get it wrong. They are reading a measure that drifted.
modules.money_connection: When more units of money are added but the goods stay the same, each unit measures less than it used to. The number on a price tag or a savings account can rise while what it buys falls. This is the plain mechanism behind what people call inflation.
modules.bitcoin_connection: You now feel the problem: a measure that can be changed by whoever runs it distorts everyone's choices. Keep that. We are almost ready to ask what a measure that nobody could quietly change would do.
modules.exit_takeaway: Money is a measuring stick for time, work, and plans. If the stick changes length, people misread reality and make decisions they would not otherwise make.

reflection.predict: Before adjusting: if a savings number went up every year but bought less food each year, is the saver getting richer?
reflection.explain: After the scenarios: why does a drifting measure cause good decisions to turn into bad ones?
reflection.compare: Compare a fixed measuring stick (a ruler) with one that quietly shrinks. Which one would you want to build plans on, and why?

modules.pattern: People kept reading the scoreboard, so when the scoreboard drifted, their good decisions quietly turned into bad ones.

modules.title: Trust, but Know What You Can Check
modules.core_question: For anything that matters: who can help you, who can block you, who can lose it, who can change the rules, what you can verify, and what responsibility comes back to you?
modules.hook: Would you give your phone passcode to your best friend? Your locker code? Your bank app login? You already sort the world by how much you trust who holds what. Let us make that sorting visible.
modules.instruction: Pick two or more holders of something valuable. Compare them across the same six questions. Notice the tradeoff: more control usually means more responsibility, not less risk.
modules.concept_text: Trust is not the enemy. We trust people and systems all the time, and that is fine. The fragile thing is blind trust, where you cannot check and cannot leave. Some systems let you verify instead of only trusting. Verifying has a price: more of the responsibility comes back to you.
modules.money_connection: Money is one of the things you have to decide who holds. A bank can help you and can also block you. Holding something yourself removes the blocker and hands you the whole responsibility, including the chance to lose it.
modules.bitcoin_connection: Bitcoin is the first place in this experience where the word appears for real. Bitcoin does not remove trust from your life. It changes where the trust sits, and it hands more responsibility back to the holder. Both the gain and the cost are real. Collaborative multisig is one setup where no single person, not even you alone, can move the money by themselves, which lowers the chance any one mistake or any one party can lose or seize it. A seed phrase is the secret backup behind holding your own keys. You never type a real one into a website, and anyone who asks you for one is trying to take what it holds.
modules.exit_takeaway: Trust is useful. Blind trust is fragile. The real question is always where the trust sits and what you can check.

reflection.predict: Before comparing: name one thing you are glad someone else holds for you. Why are you glad?
reflection.explain: After the map: in your own words, what is the difference between trusting and verifying?
reflection.compare: Compare holding something entirely yourself with a setup where no single person can act alone. What does each one cost you?

modules.pattern: No holder was simply best. Each one traded help, control, and risk differently, and more control kept coming with more responsibility.

modules.title: Bitcoin Changes One Rule
modules.core_question: If one rule of the money were fixed and public instead of adjustable, what would change downstream?
modules.hook: Back to the referee. What if the game had a scoreboard that nobody, not even the referee, could secretly edit? That is the one change we are going to study. Not magic. One rule.
modules.instruction: Switch between two systems and read each effect honestly, including the costs of the fixed-rule system. Neither column is perfect.
modules.concept_text: System A lets the supply of money change when leaders decide. System B follows a fixed, public rule that no central actor can easily change. Bitcoin is built like System B. That makes some things easier and some things harder. It is a tradeoff, not a victory.
modules.money_connection: Everything from the earlier modules meets here. Scarcity, value, incentives, a measuring stick, and trust all change depending on whether the supply rule can be quietly moved.
modules.bitcoin_connection: Bitcoin does not remove scarcity. It makes scarcity harder to hide by removing the ability to quietly change the money. It also gives more responsibility back to the holder. It is not an escape from economics. It is a system that refuses to pretend scarcity disappeared, and it asks more of you in return.
modules.exit_takeaway: Bitcoin does not remove scarcity. It makes scarcity harder to hide by removing the ability to quietly change the money. It also gives more responsibility back to the holder.

reflection.predict: Before you toggle: name one thing you expect a fixed money rule to make better.
reflection.explain: After the lab: name one thing a fixed rule makes better and one thing it makes harder.
reflection.compare: Who carries the new responsibility under System B, and is that a price you would pay? There is no right answer here.

modules.pattern: Changing one rule, who can change the supply, rippled into saving, borrowing, prices, power, and responsibility. None of it came free.

activities.label: Grab something to eat
activities.gained: You ate. You are not hungry later.
activities.gave_up: Skipping food saves money, but a hungry night has its own cost.

activities.label: Set money aside
activities.gained: You kept ten dollars in savings. It is still there next week.
activities.gave_up: Saving means this ten cannot also be spent tonight. That is the trade.

activities.label: Go out with friends
activities.gained: You spent real time with friends. That does not come back cheaply.
activities.gave_up: Going out eats the biggest chunk of your evening at once.

activities.label: Help a friend who asked
activities.gained: Your friend remembers that you showed up.
activities.gave_up: An hour helping is an hour not spent on your own list.

activities.label: Charge your phone
activities.gained: Your phone has power for later. Quiet, but it pays off.
activities.gave_up: Charging costs you half an hour now for capability you might not use.

activities.label: Finish homework
activities.gained: The work is done. Your weekend is actually free now.
activities.gave_up: Ninety minutes on homework is ninety minutes not spent on anything fun.

activities.label: Buy something online
activities.gained: You bought the thing. The money is gone now, not later.
activities.gave_up: Fifteen dollars on one purchase is most of your money for the night.

activities.label: Time with family
activities.gained: An hour with family that you will not get to redo.
activities.gave_up: Family time is calm but it is still an hour off your list.

activities.label: Leave part of the night to rest
activities.gained: You kept some of the night to rest. Not exciting, but you will feel it tomorrow.
activities.gave_up: Leaving room to rest means giving up whatever else the evening could have held.

friday_consequences.text: You did not finish it. The weekend you meant to relax turns into Sunday-night catch-up, tired and behind.

friday_consequences.fail_text: Your phone died while you were out. For a while there was no easy way to call a ride or check the time.

friday_consequences.text: After the online purchase there was not enough left to eat well. The night ended a little hungry.

objects.label: A recovery code for your account

normal.note: Nice to have. You could get water almost anywhere.

emergency.note: In a heatwave or after hours with none, water becomes the thing that matters most.

travel.note: Harder to find on the move, so it is worth carrying.

crisis.note: Small comforts carry weight when everything else is heavy.

normal.note: At full battery, a charger is almost worthless to you right now.

emergency.note: At two percent, with one call to make, a charger is suddenly priceless.

travel.note: Away from home, a dead phone is a real problem, so the charger matters.

lostphone.note: With no phone to charge, the charger does almost nothing for you.

emergency.note: Cold and stuck outside, warmth jumps up the list.

travel.note: Useful when you cannot control where you will be.

pressure.note: What you wear can carry social weight in some rooms.

normal.note: If the show is tonight and you want it, the ticket is worth a lot to you.

pressure.note: When everyone is going, the ticket feels like the whole night.

crisis.note: During a family crisis, a concert is the last thing on your mind.

emergency.note: In an emergency the ticket is just paper.

normal.note: Useful and flexible. It buys a normal-day range of things.

inflation.note: When prices rise fast, the same twenty buys less each week.

emergency.note: Cash you can use right now is worth more when options are few.

freeze.note: If your account is frozen, physical cash you hold is one of the few things that still works.

normal.note: Important, but you barely think about it day to day.

freeze.note: When access is the problem, the key to your account matters more.

pressure.note: If someone is pressuring you to share it, its value to you and to them is suddenly very different.

lostphone.note: With your phone gone, the password is what still proves the account is yours.

normal.note: Handy if you take the bus, easy to replace if not.

travel.note: When getting around is the whole problem, the pass climbs the list.

emergency.note: A way to move can matter when you need to get somewhere fast.

lostphone.note: If your phone held your bus tickets, a physical pass still gets you home when the phone is gone.

normal.note: Tempting, but it does not actually teach you anything.

pressure.note: The night before a test, with friends asking, it feels valuable. The value is mostly social, not real learning.

crisis.note: When real life is heavy, a homework answer shrinks to almost nothing.

emergency.note: In a real emergency, a homework answer is worth almost nothing.

normal.note: Sits in a drawer most of the time.

travel.note: At a border, the passport is the difference between moving and being stuck.

crisis.note: If a family has to move quickly, identity documents become critical.

freeze.note: Proof of who you are can matter when systems lock up.

normal.note: A recovery code is the backup that gets you back into a locked account. Even on a normal day it is sensitive, and you never share it.

emergency.note: Lose it and you can be locked out of your own account, sometimes for good. That is why you store it carefully and never hand it over.

freeze.note: When you are locked out and support is slow, the recovery code is what proves the account is really yours.

pressure.note: Anyone pressuring you to share a recovery code is trying to get into something that is yours. The right amount to share is zero.

incentive_rules.label: No penalty for late work
incentive_rules.immediate: Fewer assignments come in on time. Why rush when nothing changes?
incentive_rules.hidden_cost: Work piles up and feedback comes too late to help anyone learn.
incentive_rules.benefits: Students who were overloaded that week get breathing room.
incentive_rules.pays: Students who plan ahead get no reward for it, and the class falls behind together.
incentive_rules.over_time: As more people notice nothing happens, on-time work slowly becomes the exception, not the rule.

incentive_rules.label: Extra credit for finishing fastest
incentive_rules.immediate: People race. The first few hands shoot up.
incentive_rules.hidden_cost: Speed gets rewarded over care, so quality and double-checking quietly drop.
incentive_rules.benefits: Fast, confident students collect the bonus.
incentive_rules.pays: Careful students who would have done better work feel punished for thinking.
incentive_rules.over_time: The class learns that looking fast beats being right, even when being right matters more.

incentive_rules.label: Everyone gets the same grade
incentive_rules.immediate: It sounds fair and the pressure drops at first.
incentive_rules.hidden_cost: Effort stops changing the result, so effort quietly fades.
incentive_rules.benefits: People who did little get carried by people who did a lot.
incentive_rules.pays: The hardest workers get the same outcome whether they try or not, and most of them stop trying.
incentive_rules.over_time: Total effort in the class drifts down, because the rule rewards doing nothing exactly as much as doing everything.

incentive_rules.label: Only the top three students are rewarded
incentive_rules.immediate: A few students push very hard for the top spots.
incentive_rules.hidden_cost: Everyone who decides they cannot reach the top stops competing.
incentive_rules.benefits: The three strongest students get recognised.
incentive_rules.pays: The middle and the back of the class disengage, since the reward was never reachable for them.
incentive_rules.over_time: A small group sprints and a large group checks out, even though many of them could have improved a lot.

incentive_rules.label: Cheating is ignored
incentive_rules.immediate: A few people copy and get away with it.
incentive_rules.hidden_cost: Honest work starts to look like a disadvantage.
incentive_rules.benefits: Whoever cheats gets a result they did not earn, for now.
incentive_rules.pays: Students who play straight watch cheaters do better, and some of them give in to keep up.
incentive_rules.over_time: Trust in the grades collapses, and the grade stops meaning anything about what anyone learned.

incentive_rules.label: One shared grade for a group project
incentive_rules.immediate: The group celebrates, then quietly waits to see who does the work.
incentive_rules.hidden_cost: One or two people carry everyone, because slacking costs nothing.
incentive_rules.benefits: People who do little still get the group's grade.
incentive_rules.pays: The people who care most do the most and get no extra credit for it.
incentive_rules.over_time: Reliable students learn to dread group work, because the rule rewards riding along.

incentive_rules.label: Phones allowed during tests
incentive_rules.immediate: Scores jump. Almost everyone looks like they know the material.
incentive_rules.hidden_cost: The test stops measuring what anyone actually understands.
incentive_rules.benefits: Students who did not study still pass.
incentive_rules.pays: The grade becomes useless to anyone trying to learn or to anyone reading it later.
incentive_rules.over_time: Studying feels pointless, so real learning drops even as the scores stay high.

incentive_rules.label: The class project wins by classmates' votes, not by quality
incentive_rules.immediate: People start campaigning for votes instead of improving the work.
incentive_rules.hidden_cost: The best project can lose to the most-liked team, so quality stops being the goal.
incentive_rules.benefits: Well-liked or loud students collect the votes.
incentive_rules.pays: Students who quietly built the better project get beaten by a popularity contest.
incentive_rules.over_time: People learn to court votes instead of building something good.

scoreboard_scenarios.label: The referee adds points to one team mid-game
scoreboard_scenarios.true_signal: Both teams scored the same number of real points.
scoreboard_scenarios.reported_signal: The scoreboard shows one team comfortably ahead.
scoreboard_scenarios.behavior: The losing team plays desperately and the winning team coasts, all based on a number that was never earned.
scoreboard_scenarios.real_outcome: The game's result no longer reflects who actually played better.

scoreboard_scenarios.label: The teacher raises everyone's grade after the test
scoreboard_scenarios.true_signal: Some students understood the material and some did not.
scoreboard_scenarios.reported_signal: Almost everyone's report shows a high grade.
scoreboard_scenarios.behavior: Students who were struggling believe they are fine and stop getting help.
scoreboard_scenarios.real_outcome: The gap between what people know and what the grade says keeps growing until it surfaces later, painfully.

scoreboard_scenarios.label: A store changes its prices every hour
scoreboard_scenarios.true_signal: The goods on the shelf are the same as yesterday.
scoreboard_scenarios.reported_signal: The price tags say something different every time you look.
scoreboard_scenarios.behavior: Shoppers stop planning and start panic buying whenever a price looks low, because they cannot trust it to hold.
scoreboard_scenarios.real_outcome: When the number on the tag keeps moving but the goods do not, you lose your sense of what anything is really worth. The ruler changed, not the goods.

scoreboard_scenarios.label: A savings number rises while it buys less food
scoreboard_scenarios.true_signal: The amount of food the savings can buy is shrinking.
scoreboard_scenarios.reported_signal: The number in the account is going up each year.
scoreboard_scenarios.behavior: The saver feels richer and relaxes, while quietly being able to afford less.
scoreboard_scenarios.real_outcome: Years of careful saving turn out to protect far less than the rising number promised.

scoreboard_scenarios.label: More money is printed to cover a big promise
scoreboard_scenarios.true_signal: The country has the same amount of real goods and work as before.
scoreboard_scenarios.reported_signal: There is visibly more money moving around, so things feel flush for a moment.
scoreboard_scenarios.behavior: People spend into the apparent boom, then prices climb as the extra money chases the same goods.
scoreboard_scenarios.real_outcome: The promise got paid in units that each measure a little less. The cost did not disappear, it spread out quietly across everyone holding the money.

trust_questions.label: What responsibility comes back to you?

answers.help: Your friend, if they are nearby and willing.
answers.block: Your friend, if they decide not to give it back right now.
answers.lose: Your friend can drop it, misplace it, or have it taken.
answers.rules: Mostly nobody formal. It runs on your friendship.
answers.verify: You can see it in their hands and ask for it back.
answers.responsibility: Light. You picked who to trust, and you can ask anytime.

answers.help: Teachers and the office can correct or explain your record.
answers.block: The school can withhold or change access to your record.
answers.lose: A system error or lost file can damage your record.
answers.rules: The school and the district set and change the rules.
answers.verify: You can usually request to see your record, but not the full system.
answers.responsibility: Low day to day, but you depend on them being fair and careful.

answers.help: The bank can move money, reverse some mistakes, and help if a card is stolen.
answers.block: The bank can freeze or limit your account under its rules or the law.
answers.lose: Losses are usually covered up to limits, but access can still be interrupted.
answers.rules: The bank and regulators set the rules and can change them.
answers.verify: You can see a balance, but you cannot directly verify the bank's full books.
answers.responsibility: Low effort, but you are trusting the bank and the rules around it.

answers.help: The exchange can reset your login and assist with its app.
answers.block: The exchange can pause withdrawals or freeze your account.
answers.lose: If the exchange is hacked or fails, customer funds can be at risk together.
answers.rules: The exchange sets its terms and can change them.
answers.verify: You see numbers in their app, but you are trusting that the Bitcoin is really there.
answers.responsibility: Low effort, high trust. You do not hold the keys, so you cannot act alone.

answers.help: Mostly only you. There is no support line that can move it for you.
answers.block: No company can block you. That is the point.
answers.lose: You can lose it through a lost backup, a mistake, or theft, with no undo.
answers.rules: The Bitcoin network's rules apply, and no single company can change them for you.
answers.verify: You can verify your own balance and transactions directly.
answers.responsibility: High. The whole responsibility, including the chance of permanent loss, is yours.

trust_custodians.label: Several keys, no one acts alone (collaborative multisig)

answers.help: More than one party is involved, so help is possible without any one of them having full control.
answers.block: No single party can block you alone, because moving funds needs more than one key.
answers.lose: Losing one key does not lose the funds, which lowers the chance one mistake ends everything.
answers.rules: The Bitcoin network's rules apply, plus the agreed setup of who holds which key.
answers.verify: You can verify the setup and your own transactions directly.
answers.responsibility: Shared. You carry real responsibility, but not the entire single-mistake risk by yourself.

trust_custodians.label: Government currency you hold and use

answers.help: The system is widely accepted, so spending it is easy almost everywhere.
answers.block: Rules can restrict large moves, and the supply can be changed by policy.
answers.lose: Cash can be lost or stolen, and its buying power can fall over time.
answers.rules: Central authorities set monetary rules and can change the supply.
answers.verify: You can count what you hold, but you cannot verify or limit how much exists.
answers.responsibility: Low effort. You are trusting the people who manage the money's rules.

answers.help: The network confirms valid transactions without needing to trust a company.
answers.block: No central party can quietly block a valid transaction or change your balance.
answers.lose: The protocol does not lose your coins. You can still lose your own keys.
answers.rules: The rules are public and very hard to change, because they need broad agreement. Technical people call these rules the protocol.
answers.verify: Anyone can verify the rules, the supply, and the transactions, by running the software.
answers.responsibility: The protocol carries the rules. Holding the keys safely is still on you.

rule_change_dimensions.label: Saving
rule_change_dimensions.system_a: Saving can quietly lose value if the supply grows faster than goods. Holding cash can feel like a slow leak.
rule_change_dimensions.system_b: Saving is easier to trust over long periods, because no one can dilute it by making more units.
rule_change_dimensions.tradeoff: Easier saving is good, but it can also push people to hoard rather than invest. Both effects are real.

rule_change_dimensions.label: Prices
rule_change_dimensions.system_a: Prices tend to drift upward over time as the supply grows, so the measuring stick keeps shrinking.
rule_change_dimensions.system_b: The supply rule is fixed. Prices can still move with demand, production, fear, fashion, and technology. What does not move is how many units exist.
rule_change_dimensions.tradeoff: A steadier measure helps planning. But falling prices can also make people delay spending, which has its own costs.

rule_change_dimensions.label: Power
rule_change_dimensions.system_a: Power over the money sits with whoever can change the supply.
rule_change_dimensions.system_b: Power over the supply is spread out and constrained by a rule almost no one can move alone.
rule_change_dimensions.tradeoff: Spreading power limits quiet abuse. It also means no one can change the core money rule to respond to an emergency.

rule_change_dimensions.label: Responsibility
rule_change_dimensions.system_a: Much of the responsibility sits with institutions. You can mostly hand it off, for better and worse.
rule_change_dimensions.system_b: More responsibility comes back to the holder, including keeping keys safe with no undo button.
rule_change_dimensions.tradeoff: Holding responsibility gives you control and removes a safety net at the same time. That is the core trade of this whole experience.

rule_change_dimensions.label: Borrowing
rule_change_dimensions.system_a: Borrowing is often easier and cheaper, because more money can be created to lend.
rule_change_dimensions.system_b: Creating credit with no real savings behind it gets harder, since new money cannot be conjured to lend. Borrowing backed by real savings still works.
rule_change_dimensions.tradeoff: Cheap borrowing helps people start things, and also fuels debt and bubbles. Tight borrowing is steadier and slower.

rule_change_dimensions.label: Bailouts
rule_change_dimensions.system_a: Leaders can create money to rescue a failing bank, company, or budget in a crisis.
rule_change_dimensions.system_b: There is no quiet money-printing rescue, so failures are felt directly instead of spread across everyone.
rule_change_dimensions.tradeoff: Rescues can stop a panic from spreading. They can also reward bad decisions and hide costs. Steelman both sides before judging.

rule_change_dimensions.label: Planning
rule_change_dimensions.system_a: Long plans are harder, because the value of the money you are planning with keeps moving.
rule_change_dimensions.system_b: Long plans are easier to reason about, because the supply rule is fixed and public.
rule_change_dimensions.tradeoff: Predictability helps families and builders. It also removes a tool leaders sometimes use to respond to shocks.

rule_change_dimensions.label: Trust
rule_change_dimensions.system_a: You have to trust the people who can change the supply to do it wisely and honestly.
rule_change_dimensions.system_b: You trust a public rule and your ability to verify it, more than any single group of people.
rule_change_dimensions.tradeoff: A rule you can check is hard to abuse. But a rule cannot show mercy or judgment when something unexpected happens.

concepts.meaning: Someone who acts to improve their situation.
The better situation they are trying to reach.
The limited resources they spend to get there.
You cannot use the same time, money, or energy twice.
The next best option a choice forecloses.

concepts.teen: What it is worth to you
concepts.meaning: Value assigned by the person and the situation, not stored in the object.
A reward or punishment that changes what people do.
Whether a person takes the reward now or waits for more later.

concepts.teen: What tells you if it worked
concepts.meaning: The honest signal a system needs to correct mistakes.
A tool to compare, trade, save, and plan.
Money that gives confusing signals because its value or supply keeps shifting.
Depending on a person or system you do not control.
Confirming a thing yourself rather than taking it on faith.
The rule that governs how many units exist and who can change it.
A supply schedule no central actor can easily change.

misconceptions.feels_true: The word shows up around prices and banks.
misconceptions.missing: Economics is about any choice under a limit, including time, attention, and energy.
misconceptions.better: Economics is the study of how people act when they cannot have everything.
misconceptions.example: Deciding whether to sleep or finish a game is an economic choice with no money in it.

misconceptions.think: Value is the same for everyone.
misconceptions.feels_true: A price looks like a fixed label on the object.
misconceptions.missing: A price is where two different valuations met, not a property of the object.
misconceptions.better: The object has properties. People assign value based on their situation.
misconceptions.example: Water is nearly free at lunch and precious after a two hour practice. Same water.

misconceptions.think: If something is free, it has no cost.
misconceptions.missing: The cost is the next best thing you gave up to get it.
misconceptions.better: Every choice has an opportunity cost, even free ones.
misconceptions.example: A free three hour stream costs you the three hours you could have spent on anything else.

misconceptions.think: If the rules sound fair, the outcomes will be fair.
misconceptions.missing: Rules create incentives, and incentives, not intentions, drive outcomes.
misconceptions.better: Judge a rule by the behavior it rewards over time, not by how it sounds.
misconceptions.example: Everyone gets the same group grade sounds fair and quietly rewards the people who do nothing.

misconceptions.feels_true: More money looks like more resources.
misconceptions.missing: Money is a measuring stick, not the goods. More units chasing the same goods changes the measure, not the wealth.
misconceptions.better: You cannot make a class smarter by raising everyone's grade. You cannot make a country richer just by adding units.
misconceptions.example: If a game's referee doubles everyone's points, no one actually played better.

misconceptions.think: Banks always have everyone's money ready.
misconceptions.feels_true: The app shows your full balance instantly.
misconceptions.missing: Most deposited money is lent out, so not everyone can withdraw at once.
misconceptions.better: The balance is a promise that usually holds and occasionally does not.
misconceptions.example: If every student tried to grab their locker contents in the same minute, the hallway would not fit them.

misconceptions.feels_true: The word trustless gets repeated a lot.
misconceptions.missing: Bitcoin removes the need to trust certain parties by letting you verify, but it adds responsibility you now carry yourself.
misconceptions.better: Bitcoin moves trust, it does not delete it.
misconceptions.example: Holding your own keys means no one can block you and no one will rescue you if you lose them.

misconceptions.feels_true: No third party can fail you.
misconceptions.missing: The risk moves to you, which is why middle setups like collaborative multisig exist.
misconceptions.better: Every custody choice is a tradeoff between who can help you and who can harm you.
misconceptions.example: A diary only you can open is also a diary only you can lose.

next_steps.label: First Principles
next_steps.note: Rebuild Bitcoin from the ground up. The natural next deep dive.

next_steps.label: Money and Banking
next_steps.note: Go deeper on how money and banks actually work.

next_steps.label: Why Money Fails
next_steps.note: Is there one mechanism behind every monetary collapse? With a purchasing-power calculator.

next_steps.label: Digital Scarcity
next_steps.note: What a fixed supply really is, and the honest case against it.

next_steps.label: Explore the demos
next_steps.note: Hands-on labs at intro depth, including wallets and custody basics.

next_steps.label: For older students and families
next_steps.note: An adult and family branch. Only if you are older or doing this with your family.

claim_ledger.claim: Bitcoin follows a fixed supply rule that no central actor can easily change, with issuance on a public schedule that halves over time toward a capped total.
claim_ledger.note: Describe the rule and the halving schedule as mechanism. Do not quote a current block subsidy figure or any price, which age quickly. Verify against a primary source before publication.
claim_ledger.verified_note: 21 million cap and the 210,000-block (about four year) halving schedule are enforced by every full node via consensus rules. Copy describes the rule and halving as mechanism and quotes no current block subsidy or price, both of which age.

claim_ledger.claim: Adding more units of money while the amount of real goods stays the same makes each unit measure less, which is the plain mechanism behind rising prices.
claim_ledger.note: Framed as the standard quantity intuition. Keep cautious wording until cited against a primary source.
verified_sources: St. Louis Fed Feducation: Money and Inflation
claim_ledger.verified_note: Standard quantity-theory framing: when money grows faster than goods and services, prices rise. Kept at mechanism level.

claim_ledger.claim: Banks lend out most deposited money rather than holding all of it in reserve, so not every depositor can withdraw at the same time.
preferred_sources: central bank explainer on fractional reserve banking
claim_ledger.note: Mechanism only. No named institution. Verify the general description against a primary source before publication.
verified_sources: Federal Reserve / FDIC educational material on fractional reserve banking and bank runs
claim_ledger.verified_note: Banks hold only a fraction of deposits as reserves and lend or invest the rest, so a sudden mass withdrawal can exceed available cash. Mechanism only, no named institution. Note: US reserve requirements were set to zero in 2020, so framing is liquidity-of-deposits, not a fixed reserve ratio.

claim_ledger.claim: A seed phrase controls a Bitcoin wallet, and losing or sharing it can mean losing what it holds.
claim_ledger.note: Used only to justify never entering a real seed phrase. Safe framing, still mark for a source check.
verified_sources: BIP-39 (Mnemonic code for generating deterministic keys)
claim_ledger.verified_note: A seed phrase (BIP-39 mnemonic) derives the keys that control a wallet. Used only to justify never entering a real one. The deep dive never accepts seed-phrase input.

hub.lede: Choices, incentives, money, and Bitcoin. Not a lecture. Six short tools where you make a choice, see what it costs, and only then put a name to the idea. It starts with your Friday night, not with banks.
hub.start_text: You have twenty dollars, three hours, and a phone battery at forty percent. Homework is due tomorrow. You cannot do everything you want to do tonight. That small, ordinary problem is the door into everything else here, all the way to what makes Bitcoin different.
hub.how_text: Six modules, about twenty to thirty five minutes in total. In each one you act first and the principle is named afterward. There are no quizzes, no grades, no points, no badges, and nothing fills up to one hundred percent. You can stop and come back anytime. The reflection boxes are optional and stay on your own device.
hub.glossary_intro: Every idea in this deep dive has a plain name and a more formal one. Here is the whole set in one place. You do not need to memorize any of it. You will meet each one by doing, not by reading.
hub.misconception_intro: These are the ideas that feel true but quietly mislead. Each card says what people often think, why it feels right, what is missing, and a clearer way to see it.
hub.label_better: A better way to see it
synthesis_paragraphs: By the end you should be able to say, in your own words, why scarcity means choice, why every choice has a cost, why value depends on the person and the situation, why people follow incentives, why money works like a scoreboard, why a scoreboard that can be quietly changed distorts decisions, and why trust needs limits.

And one careful claim about Bitcoin, earned rather than asserted: Bitcoin does not remove scarcity. It makes scarcity harder to hide by removing the ability to quietly change the money. It also gives more responsibility back to the holder. That is a tradeoff, not a magic fix, and the last module shows both sides.
hub.next_intro: All free. Follow whichever question you are still chewing on.
hub.parent_label: A note for parents, teachers, and facilitators.
hub.parent_text: This works as a discussion guide. Let the student make a choice before you talk about the principle. The reflection prompts (predict, explain, compare) are good conversation starters. Nothing here gives financial, legal, or tax advice, and nothing asks for any private or sensitive information.
hub.reflect_title: Reflect: which rule under the game surprised you most?
